semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek are proper names. Doukas was a Byzantine family name (related to Latin dux = leader). Arabic rendering of a proper name. Excluded as proper names.
key_insight: Both are proper names (Byzantine Doukas family vs Arabic rendering) - excluded as proper names.

semantic_journey: Arabic sabata = to rest, cease activity, the Semitic root s-b-t meaning cessation/rest. Greek sabbaton = Sabbath (day of rest). Greek borrowed sabbaton from Hebrew/Aramaic shabbat (to rest/cease). The Semitic root s-b-t encoding rest/cessation is genuine, and Greek received the term via Semitic. Confirmed Semitic religious-cultural term transmission.
key_insight: Semitic rest/cessation root (s-b-t) entered Greek as sabbaton - genuine Semitic religious-cultural term transmission.

semantic_journey: Arabic patterned inlay/banding on cloth or ground (generic). Greek diakhrysos = interwoven specifically with gold (gold-threaded). The Arabic concept of generic patterned inlay -> Greek specifically gold-threaded requires knowing the metal, which is an additional unspecified step. Also phonetics are distant. False positive.
key_insight: Arabic generic patterning does not specifically connect to Greek gold-threading - specificity mismatch.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-harth = tillage/ploughing (agricultural action). Greek helkethron = the stock (wooden frame) of a plough, derives from Greek helko (to drag/pull). The Greek word is native Greek (pull/drag -> dragging implement), not from a Semitic root. Phonetics are very distant. False cognates.
key_insight: Greek helkethron derives from native Greek helko (to drag) - not from Arabic ploughing action.

semantic_journey: Arabic subsiding/calming/settling (becoming still). Greek hesukhazetai = be quiet, rest, be at peace. The Arabic root carries the sense of subsidence/calming; Greek derives from hesukhos (quiet/still). The semantic core is identical: becoming quiet/still/calm. Both encode transition from activity to stillness. One direct step.
key_insight: Arabic subsiding/calming aligns with Greek being-quiet/still - direct stillness concept.

semantic_journey: Arabic and Greek are proper names. Sybrita was a pre-Greek settlement in Crete with a name of uncertain (possibly pre-Greek Aegean) origin. Arabic form is a rendering of a toponym. Excluded as proper names.
key_insight: Sybrita is a Cretan city name of pre-Greek origin - proper name, no Arabic root connection.

semantic_journey: Arabic barakan = a black cloak or outer garment. Greek barakine = a type of garment/cloth (attested in late Greek sources). Both name a specific garment or cloth type. The Arabic root b-r-k-n names a black outer cloak; Greek barakine names a similar cloth/garment. Plausible Semitic garment term that entered Greek, or shared substrate. The clothing type and name correspondence are close enough for plausible material culture transmission.
key_insight: Arabic black cloak (barakan) and Greek garment (barakine) plausibly share a material culture garment term.

semantic_journey: Root confusion in the original reasoning: Arabic al-saatar is the herb thyme/savory, not a rogue. The original reasoning conflates al-saatar (thyme) with al-shater (clever rogue). If we use al-saatar (thyme), there is no connection to Greek female robber. If we use al-shater, the chain is still too long. Root misidentification makes this a false positive.
key_insight: Root confusion: Arabic thyme (al-saatar) has no connection to Greek female robber - root mismatch in original scoring.
